Certainly. Here's a letter written in the voice of a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper, addressing his estranged daughter with a mix of regret, resolve, and hope. The tone is somber, introspective, and deeply human.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

It has been five long years since I last saw your face, and five years since I last heard your voice. Time has a way of slipping through the cracks of the world, like the sea slipping through the cracks of a crumbling cliff. I write this with a trembling hand, knowing that the storm that is coming may not be the last I see of this world.

You were always a child of the sea, Eliza. You were born beneath the waves, and I was the one who watched over them. I was the one who made sure the light shone through the darkness, so that no ship would be lost to the night. But now, I fear I have become more of a shadow than a man.

I do not know what has become of you. I have not heard from you in five years, and I have not written to you. I suppose I was afraid that the words might not be enough to mend what had already been broken. But tonight, as the wind howls through the stone walls of this lighthouse and the sea roars like a beast, I feel the weight of silence pressing down on me. I think, for the first time in years, I

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The storm is upon us, and the sea howls like a wounded beast. I have spent my life standing firm against the elements, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to hold on.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard from you. I have tried to forgive, but the silence between us has grown colder than the wind that howls through these cliffs. I have often wondered if you still remember me at all.

I have always believed that the sea has a way of bringing things to light, even when we are too stubborn to look. I suppose I was too proud to reach out, too afraid that my words would not be enough. But now, as the waves rise and the sky darkens, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were once my greatest pride, and I hoped that you would carry the light of our family with you. But I fear I have failed you, and I do not know if I can ever make it right. I have spent so long in this lighthouse, watching the world pass by, and I have come to understand that sometimes the light we shine is not enough to reach those who have turned away.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even care. But I must say it, for I have no one else to speak to. I have always believed

**April 15th, 1894**  
**Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea, that ancient and unrelenting thing, roars with fury. I fear the night will not end kindly, and I have no doubt that I shall not see dawn. But I must write—must say what I have not said in five years, or perhaps never again.

You have not written to me. Not once. I know you would not, for I have done the same. We are both proud, stubborn things. But I have thought of you. I have dreamed of you. I have wept in the silence of this lighthouse, for I have not spoken to my daughter in five years.

I do not know if you will read this. Perhaps the sea will claim me before the letter reaches you. But if it does, I have no regrets. I have lived a life of duty, of sacrifice, of quiet endurance. I have stood alone on this rock for more than twenty years, watching the waves and the stars, and I have never once felt more alone than I do now, knowing you do not call me father.

I have not been a good father. I have been a man consumed by the sea, by the light, by the responsibility that is heavier than the stones of this lighthouse. I have not been

**My Dearest Eliza,**

Five years have passed since you left this lonely tower, and yet the sea still whispers your name with every crashing wave. I have not spoken to you in that time, not in words, not in letters—though I have written many, and sent them to the sea, where they were swallowed by the tide like the dreams I once had of you.

The storm is coming, Eliza. Not the kind that merely rattles the windows or douses the lamp. This is a tempest of the soul, a howling gale that has been building in my chest for decades. The wind has been rising all night, and the waves, they grow louder, fiercer—like the sea is trying to tell me something I have long since stopped listening to.

You were always the storm, my child. The first time you came to visit, you brought with you a wind that blew through the very bones of this place. You laughed at the idea of a lighthouse keeper being afraid of the dark, and I—old, stubborn, and proud—let you see the light. But the light is not enough, not when the darkness is not of the sea, but of the heart.

I have kept the lamp burning these past years, not for the ships, but for the memory of your voice. I have watched the sky turn to ink and the waves rise like the hands of God, and I have thought of you. I have thought

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, on a night when the sky is dark as ink and the wind howls like the devil himself. The sea is restless, and I fear the storm that is coming may be more than nature can bear. I have spent many years watching the waves and the stars, and I have seen many storms. But tonight, I feel something different—something that stirs in my bones and tells me that this may be the end.

I have not written to you in five years, and I have not spoken to you in even longer. You have been a ghost in my life, a shadow that haunts my every thought. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to reach you, but you have turned your back on me. I do not know why. I do not know if it was my fault or if it was yours. But I do know this: I have always loved you, and I have always tried to protect you.

You were a child when I first saw you, a little girl with wide eyes and a heart full of wonder. I took you in when your mother died, and I raised you as my own. I taught you the ways of the sea, the rhythms of the tides, and the silence of the night. I gave you everything I had, and I expected you to return the favor. But you did not. You left, and you never came back.

I have been a lighthouse

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the light, the sea howling like a wounded beast, and my hands tremble as I write. The wind howls through the tower, and I fear it may be the last time I shall hear the sea's voice, or see the dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I shall be left with the weight of silence.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. I have not seen you, nor heard from you. I have tried to write, but the ink would not flow, and the letters would not reach you. I suppose I was not worthy of your words, or perhaps you thought I was too much of a fool to understand what you were going through.

But I have been here, alone, for all these years. The light has been my companion, the sea my only friend. I have kept the beacon burning, as I always have, even when the world seemed to forget me. And now, as the storm gathers, I wonder if I have been wrong to keep it burning, if I have been too stubborn to let the world in.

I have no regrets, not truly. I have done my duty, as I always have. But I have missed you, Eleanor. I have missed the sound of your voice, the sight of your face, the warmth of your presence. I have missed you more than I can say.

If I do not survive this night,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write these words with a trembling hand and a heart that aches with more than mere sorrow. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the years have worn away the edges of our words, they have not softened the weight of the silence between us. Tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very sky, I find myself questioning whether I shall live to see the dawn. And if I do, I wonder if you will still be here to hear the truth of what I have to say.

You were once my greatest joy, Eliza. I remember the day you first came to me, all wide-eyed and full of questions, as if the world were a puzzle you were determined to solve. You were curious, bright, and full of the fire of youth. I loved you with a love that was more than fatherly—it was the kind that a man feels for the daughter who looks back at him with the eyes of a child and the soul of a poet. But time, as it often does, brought with it changes we could not foresee.

I know you left, and I know it was not by choice. You said you needed space, that you were searching for something greater than what we could offer. I did not stop you. I did not try to hold you back. And for that, I bear no regret. But I have not forgotten the

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now, in the quiet of this storm-laden night, with the wind howling like a wounded beast and the sea roaring with fury. The lighthouse stands, though I fear it may not stand much longer. I have seen the sky turn to a dark, brooding mass, and the waves rise higher than ever before. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, and I write this not only as a man of duty but as a father who has longed for your return.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and though I have not spoken to you, I have not ceased to think of you. I have often sat upon the cliffs, watching the sea and the sky, wondering what you might be doing, what thoughts you might have, and whether you still remember the man I once was. I have thought of the days we spent together, the laughter, the warmth, and the love that once filled this house.

I have tried, in my own way, to be the kind of father you deserved. I have raised you in the best way I could, and though I may have made mistakes, I have always tried to do right by you. I know I may not have always been the man you needed, but I have never stopped loving you.

I have not written to you for so long, and I do not know if you will read these words, or if you will even believe them

**The Lighthouse, Stonehaven**  
**October 31st, 1897**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the ink slightly blurred by the salt and the rain, though the rain has yet to come. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it too is crying. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since you left, though I do not know if it will be seen by any ship or soul this evening.

Five years have passed since you walked out of this house, leaving the door ajar, as if you had never meant to return. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, not even in the silence of the night when the waves crash against the cliffs and the world feels so very small.

I have kept the lighthouse, as you once told me I would. I have tended the light, though I no longer know if it serves any purpose beyond my own stubbornness. The sea is fickle, and the storms come without warning. Tonight, it seems, the sea has chosen to remind me of what I have lost.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. Not a single day. I have watched the stars from the tower, and I have thought of you beneath them. I have seen the moon rise and set, just as you once did, and I have wondered if you ever looked up and saw the same light I keep burning.

I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that looms on the horizon. Five years have passed since you left, and though I have not spoken to you, I have not forgotten. Each day, the sea whispers your name, and the wind carries echoes of your footsteps along the shore. Tonight, the storm is unlike any we have seen in decades, and I fear I may not see dawn.

I have spent these years in silence, but not in regret. I have kept the lighthouse burning, not for the sea, but for you. Every beam that cuts through the darkness, every light that guides the lost, is a prayer that you might one day return.

I know you think I have no heart, that I have turned my back on you. But I have never turned my back on the sea. It has always been my only companion, my only truth. You left with your father’s pride, and I have kept the lighthouse, as he would have wished.

I do not know if I will survive the night, but if I do, I will not be alone. The sea is fierce, but it is also faithful. And so, if you ever come back, I will be here, waiting. Not with anger, but with the quiet hope that you might still remember who I am.

**Your father**  
*P.S. The light still burns. Always has. Always will.*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the lantern’s light flickering in the wind, the sea howling like a beast in pain. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet I must speak, for there are words I have long kept buried, and time is short.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a single word from you. I know you believe I have forgotten you, that I have turned my back on you, but that is not true. I have carried your name in my heart every day, though I have not seen your face for so long. I have watched the waves crash against these rocks, and in their rhythm, I have heard your voice, though I cannot say what it says.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and I have tried to fulfill my role as a lighthouse keeper with honor. But I have also been a fool, for I let pride and silence keep me from reaching out to you. I know you were hurt, and I understand that pain, for I have felt it too. But I pray that you may one day see that I never stopped loving you.

The world is cruel, and I have seen many storms in my years here. But this one feels different. The wind is fierce, the sea is wild, and I fear that this night may be my last. If I do not survive, I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds me in a state of quiet desolation. Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. Time has not softened the ache of your absence, nor has it mended the rift between us. I’ve tried to write to you many times, but my hands have always trembled too much, my voice too hoarse, and my heart too full of things I’ve never been able to say.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sea roars like a beast waking from its slumber, and the wind howls through the cracks in the lighthouse like a ghost demanding to be heard. I’ve lit the beacon, as I always do, and the light cuts through the darkness like a blade. It’s the only thing that keeps me from going mad. The only thing that keeps me from thinking of you.

I’ve thought of you more than I care to admit. I’ve thought of the way you used to run up the spiral stairs, your laughter echoing through the stone walls, and the way you’d insist on helping me clean the lens, even though you were too small to reach the top. You were a fire in those days, Eliza. A flame that burned too bright, too fierce, and I was too proud to tell you that I feared it.

Certainly! Here's a letter from a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper to his estranged daughter, written in the tone of someone who is both weary and regretful, yet still holding on to hope. The storm is coming, and he's not sure he'll survive it—but he wants to make sure she knows he's still here, even if she doesn't want to hear from him.

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea is restless tonight, and the light must be kept burning. I've been watching the waves for hours, and I can feel the storm coming. It’s not just the sea that threatens us—this night is heavy with something deeper, something more dangerous than the tide.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of a heart that refuses to let go. I’ve tried to forget, to move on, to find peace in the solitude of this lighthouse. But the sea doesn’t forget, and neither do I.

I know you don’t want to hear from me. You never did. You said I was too stern, too distant, too much like the sea itself—cold, unfeeling, and always watching. I can still hear your words, clear as the morning mist. I was never the father you wanted. I was never the man you needed.

But I am still here.

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, and the sea raging as if it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the darkness closes in, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my only child.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. You have turned your back on me, as you have turned your back on so many others. But I cannot let this night pass without saying what I must. You were once my light, my guiding star. Now, I fear I have become a shadow in your life, a man who has lost his way.

I have spent these years in solitude, tending to this lighthouse, watching the sea with weary eyes. The light burns bright, but it is not the same as the warmth of a mother’s embrace or the laughter of a child. I have thought of you often, and I have prayed that one day, you might come back. But time has passed, and I fear I may not live to see that day.

There is no grand excuse, no great sorrow, only the quiet truth that I have failed you. I have failed to be the father you deserved. I have let my pride and my stubbornness stand between us. I have not been there when you needed me

**April 15th, 1878**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that feels as heavy as the storm clouds that gather on the horizon. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the land whole. I have not seen the light in years, and yet tonight, it seems the very heavens have conspired to remind me of the one thing I have always been unable to let go of—**you**.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have watched the waves crash against these rocks, and I have watched the days slip by like sand through my fingers. I have spoken to no one but the sea and the wind, and the only voice that has ever answered mine was the one I chose to silence.

You were a child when you left. A child with a heart full of fire and a mind full of dreams. I saw it in your eyes, that same fire that burned in my own. But you did not stay, and I did not stop you. I told myself I was doing what was best. I told myself I was a father, not a jailer. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was a fool, and I have been a fool for five long years.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the wind howling like a vengeful specter across the cliffs, the sea roiling in a fury that seems almost human. The lamps have long since been extinguished, and the storm has taken the last of the light from the tower. I fear I may not see dawn.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. Not a single word. You were but a child when you ran from this place, from me, from the life I tried to give you. I told myself I would not chase you, that I would let the sea decide who was worthy of its light. But now, as the waves crash against the rocks with a sound like thunder, I find I have no one left to keep the lamp alight.

You were always so quick to leave, my dear. You thought the world was yours to command, that you could walk away from a father's love and call it a lesson. But I have watched you vanish into the fog, and the fog has only grown thicker since.

I have kept the lighthouse, as I have kept my silence. The work is endless, the solitude a burden I have learned to bear. The villagers speak of me as if I were a ghost, a man who has long since given up on the world. But I have not given up on the light. I have not given up on you.

But now, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you. Of the way you used to stand at the edge of the cliff, eyes fixed on the horizon, as if searching for something beyond the sea. I used to wonder if you were looking for me, or if you were simply lost in your own thoughts.

I have been a fool, Eleanor. I have been blind, and I have been cruel. I have let pride and silence drive a wedge between us that could not be mended. **Dear Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I hope you are not reading it, for I fear I may not live to see the morning. The storm is coming, and I have no doubt it will be the end of me.

I have written this letter with a trembling hand, and I have spent the last few nights preparing for what may come. I have lit the lamp and cleaned the lens, and I have made sure the light shines as it should, for the sea is restless and the winds are wild.

Five years have passed since you walked out of our house that last time, and I have not spoken to you since. I have not written, nor have I sent a single letter. I have not had the courage to reach out. But tonight, as I sit here with the storm raging outside, I feel a strange sense of peace. My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is a sheet of black, laced with lightning that flashes like the eyes of some ancient beast. I have not seen you in five years, and yet, in this moment, the weight of that silence presses upon me like the tide against the rocks.

I have spent many nights staring into the abyss of the sea, wondering if I have done enough to keep the light burning. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I fear that even the light may not be enough. I do not know if I will survive this night, Eliza. I have always believed that the light must never go out, but what if it does?

You were the light in my life, once. I remember the day you left, the way you turned your back to me, your voice sharp with disappointment. I thought I had failed you, that I had not been the father you deserved. I told myself that you would not come back, and perhaps I was right. But I have not stopped hoping.

I have thought of you often, in the quiet hours when the world is still. I have imagined what your life might be like, and I wonder if I have been a fool to think that I could ever truly understand you. You were always so different, so full of fire and

**October 12th, 1889**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  
**England**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with the ink still wet from the last letter I sent, though it seems a lifetime has passed since then. The sea howls at my window like a wounded beast, and the wind has grown so fierce that it rattles the panes as though the very walls might be torn from their moorings. I have not slept, and I do not know if I shall. But I must write, for I fear this may be the last night I have to spare.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years, and I have not spoken a word to you. I have not seen you. I have not even heard your voice. You were so young then, no older than I am now, and yet you left me with such a hollow heart. I have not forgotten the day you walked out that door. The way you turned without looking back, as though you had never been my daughter at all.

I know you do not forgive me. I know you never have. And I do not blame you. I have no right to expect your forgiveness. I have been a failure as a father, a husband, and a man. I have spent my days staring at the sea, not because I love it, but because it keeps me from thinking about the things I

By the flickering light of the oil lamp, I pen these words with trembling hands—both from the wind that howls through the cliffs and from the weight of years unspoken. Five years, lass, five long, bitter years since you turned your back on this rock and the life it has given me. I’ve kept the light burning for you, though I fear it may be the last time.

The sea is restless tonight, and the storm that brews on the horizon is no mere squall. It is the kind that takes lives, that swallows ships whole and leaves not a whisper of their song. I’ve seen such storms before, when I was young and thought myself invincible. But time, like the tide, has its own cruel rhythm, and I am no longer the man I was.

I have no regrets, not truly. You were always the light in my life, the one thing that kept me from drowning in the solitude of this place. But you left, as you always did, with your sharp tongue and colder heart. You said I was a fool to stay, that the sea was not worth the sorrow it brought me. And so you went, and I remained.

But tonight, as the wind screams like a banshee and the waves claw at the cliffs, I find myself wondering if I have been the fool all along. The light is dimming, the lamp’s wick nearly spent. I’ve lit it many times before, but

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heart full of sorrow and a hand that trembles from the cold. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury, as if the very heavens are in revolt. I have kept the light burning all night, though I know it may not be enough to guide the ships through this tempest. And yet, I cannot stop. Not now. Not when the world is so very still.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and though I have not seen you, I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way your eyes would light up when you saw me, or the way your voice would soften when you spoke of your dreams. I have not forgotten the way you left, nor the way you never came back.

I have been alone here, on this lonely rock, with only the sea for company. The nights are long, and the silence is deafening. But I have not given up. I have kept the light burning, as I always have. And I have kept hope, even when the world seemed to have turned its back on me.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years. The waves are higher, the wind is fiercer, and the darkness is deeper than I have ever known. I do not know if I will make it through this night. But if I do, I will write again. If I do not

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea raging with a fury I fear it cannot contain. Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of solitude, of watching the sea from my tower, and of wondering if you would ever return. I have not spoken your name in so long, and yet it lingers in my thoughts like the scent of the sea on a summer's night.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. You have always been so proud, so certain of your path, and I, a simple lighthouse keeper, was not worthy of your time. But I must write, for I fear I may not have the chance to speak again.

The storm is coming, and I have no one to call upon. The light must be kept burning, as it always has been, and I have done so for thirty years. But tonight, I do not know if my hands will be steady, if my eyes will be clear, if the wind will not claim me before the light is extinguished.

I have no regrets, Eleanor. I have lived a life of purpose, of service, of quiet duty. I have watched the world pass by, and I have kept the light burning for those who dared to sail the dark waters. But I

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years, my love, and still I have not found the words to reach you. Tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars its fury, I find myself staring into the storm, not with fear, but with the weight of unspoken truths pressing upon me like the very waves that crash against these rocks.

You were once my greatest joy, my little light in the darkness. I remember the day you came to me, a child no taller than the lighthouse itself, with eyes like the sea at dawn and a spirit that burned brighter than the beacon I guard. I promised you a home, a life of quiet dignity, and a father who would never fail you. But the world is not so kind, and I have failed you in ways no man should.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Clara. That silence is not born of cruelty, but of regret. I have spent those years in bitter reflection, trying to understand what I did, what I did not do, and what I could have done to mend the distance between us. I have come to see that the love I gave you was not enough. It was not enough to keep you safe, not enough to keep you from turning away. And I fear, with the storm upon us, that I may not have the chance to say it again

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I can't help but wonder if you've been reading it at all. Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have often thought of you in those long, lonely hours by the light. The sea is ever constant, as is the light, and so I find myself writing to you once more, not with the hope of changing what has been, but with the hope that you may one day understand.

The wind howls with a ferocity I have not seen in many a year, and the sea roars like a beast in pain. The storm is coming, and I fear for my life. I have done my duty, as I always have, and I have no regrets. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, through the darkest nights and the most treacherous seas. But tonight, I fear that the light may not be enough to save me.

I know you have not come to visit, and I know you have not written, and I know that your silence has been deafening. I have thought of you often, and I have tried to understand the distance that has grown between us. I have tried to be the father you needed, but I fear that I was not always the one you wanted.

I have no doubt that you have led your own life, your own path, and I have no right to interfere. But I do miss you, Eleanor

**The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point**  
**April 3rd, 1897**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading this, then perhaps I have not survived the night. Or perhaps you have come to the lighthouse, as you once did, with your little lantern in hand, and found me still standing here, though the light may have long since gone out.  

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out of the house with your mother, your father’s coat over your shoulders, and your eyes like distant stars. I remember the way the wind howled that night, as if the very sea were weeping. You never looked back.  

I have not spoken to you since. Not in letters, not in words, not even in the silence that has grown between us. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, for you. For the sea. For the ones who sail upon it, who rely on the glow of a single lantern to guide them home.  

Tonight, the storm is coming. The skies are black as ink, and the waves rise like the wrath of some ancient thing. I have lit the lamp, and I have prayed. Not to God, but to the sea, to the wind, to the stars that have always watched over this place. I have prayed that you would not come, that you would not see me here, still waiting, still holding the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the storm raging outside, the wind howling like a wounded beast, and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I’ve spent the last five years waiting for the right moment to reach out, and now, as the sky darkens and the waves grow wilder, I feel the time has come.

You were only ten when you left. I remember the way your eyes filled with tears as you clung to your mother’s coat, whispering, *“I’ll come back, Papa.”* I believed you then. I still do. But the years have passed, and I have not seen you since.

I’ve kept the lighthouse as you would have wanted—its light always burning, its beams reaching out to guide the lost and the lonely. It’s been my only companion, my only purpose. I’ve watched the tides rise and fall, the moon wax and wane, and the seasons change, but never you. Not once.

I don’t know if you’ll read this, or if you’ll even believe it. But I must say it anyway. I’ve never stopped thinking of you. I’ve never stopped loving you. You were my heart, and I have carried that pain like a shadow for far too long.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 5th, 1878**

**Lighthouse, St. Agnes Head**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I write this with a heart heavy as the storm that now gathers on the horizon. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with a fury that makes me fear for my life. Yet, in this moment of peril, I find myself thinking of you, and I must write, for I fear I may not have the chance to speak again.

I know not what you think of me, nor what you may feel toward me, but I write this with the hope that you might one day read these words and understand the truth of my heart. I have not forgotten you, Eliza. Though the years have passed and the distance between us has grown, my love for you remains steadfast, though perhaps it has grown colder with time.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper’s duty is to guide others safely through the darkest nights. But tonight, I find myself wondering if I have failed you. You were but a child when you left, and I could not bear the thought of you being lost to the sea or the cold. I thought I was protecting you by keeping you from the world, but perhaps I only pushed you away.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just my life.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this place, this life, and this father. I have not heard from you, nor have I written. I have no reason to, for I know you would not care. But tonight—tonight, I feel the weight of the years like a stone in my chest, and I must speak.

You were a child when you left. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too proud to ask for anything but what she wanted. I tried to stop you, but you were gone before I could say more than a curse. I did not mean it, not truly. But I said it, and you heard it, and it was enough.

You were angry, and I was ashamed. I had no right to be angry with you, but I was. You left me with only the lighthouse, the sea, and the ghosts of the men I once knew. You left me with the silence of a house that once echoed with your laughter, now hollow and cold.

I’ve kept the lighthouse. I’ve kept the light burning, as I always have. It’s the only thing I’ve ever been good for,
